front: Work: an Iliad-inspired epic poem in which the fishermen Achille and Hector fight over Helen on St. Lucia
Work: the blind poet Seven Seas frequents Ma Kilman's No Pain Cafe in this epic poem
Work: Major Plunkett tries to write a history of the island; his wife Maud dies
Work: Ma Kilman heals Philoctete's wound in this epic poem
Work: Achille paints 'In God we Troust' on his boat and sails to Africa
Work: uses a three-line form reminiscent of Dante's terza rima; has no single protagonist
Work: ends 'How can I face such slaughter and be cool? / How can I turn from Africa and live?'
back: A Far Cry from Africa (Derek Walcott)

front: Work: contains 'the gorilla wrestles with the superman'
back: A Far Cry from Africa (Derek Walcott)

front: Work: speaker is 'divided to the vein'; torn between 'this Africa and the English tongue I love'
back: A Far Cry from Africa (Derek Walcott)

front: Work: opens with 'a wind ruffling the tawny pelt' of Africa; references 'Kikuyu, quick as flies'
back: A Far Cry from Africa (Derek Walcott)

front: Work: 'brutish necessity wipes its hands upon the napkin of a dirty cause'
back: A Far Cry from Africa (Derek Walcott)

front: Work: ends 'The classics can console. But not enough.'
Work: describes 'the ancient war between obsession and responsibility'
Work: 'great hexameters come to the conclusions of exhausted surf'
Work: compares an adulterer/Odysseus hearing Nausicaa's name 'in every gull's outcry'; 'a sail which leans on light / tired of islands'
Work: charcoal-burner Makak (Felix Hobain) imprisoned by Corporal Lestrade with criminals Tigre and Souris
Work: a white goddess urges Makak to return to Africa; he beheads the goddess before waking
Work: Moustique is beaten to death by a mob for pretending to be a healer
Work: Makak heals Josephus's snakebite by pressing a coal into his palm
Work: Florence Nightingale, Al Jolson, and Horatio Nelson are sentenced to hang for being white
Work: 'disjecta membra' of the title structure; 'leprosy of empire'; 'smell of dead limes'
back: Ruins of a Great House (Derek Walcott)

front: Work: references 'men like Hawkins, Walter Raleigh, Drake, ancestral murderers and poets'
back: Ruins of a Great House (Derek Walcott)

front: Work: Bolom (ghost of an aborted fetus) challenges three brothers to vex the Devil or be eaten; Ti-Jean wins by burning a plantation and castrating a goat
Work: 'you will love again the stranger who was your self'; 'greet yourself arriving at your own door'
Work: this poem provided the title for Ngugi wa Thiong'o's Petals of Blood
Work: Shabine declares 'I have Dutch, [a slur], and English in me, and either I'm nobody, or I'm a nation'
Work: includes this poet's own watercolors and a narrative about Camille Pissarro moving to Paris
Author: had a famous feud with V.S. Naipaul; attacked his 'self-disfiguring sneers' in a review of The Enigma of Arrival
Author: collaborated with Paul Simon on the widely-panned musical The Capeman
Author: twin brother Roderick was also a playwright; co-founded the Trinidad Theatre Workshop
Author: Ruth Padel resigned as Oxford Professor of Poetry after leaking harassment charges against this poet
